questions.question: The War of 1812 between the United States and Britain is sometimes called the 'Second War of American Independence.' What notable event occurred in Washington, D.C. during this conflict?
options: The British navy blockaded the Chesapeake Bay for three years
British forces captured and burned Washington, D.C., including the White House and Capitol
President Madison was captured by British forces and held as a prisoner of war
American forces invaded and occupied London briefly during a naval raid
questions.answer: British forces captured and burned Washington, D.C., including the White House and Capitol
questions.explanation: In August 1814, British forces marched into Washington, D.C. and set fire to the White House, the Capitol, and other federal buildings. First Lady Dolley Madison famously saved a portrait of George Washington before fleeing. The burning of Washington remains the only time since the Revolutionary War that a foreign power has captured and occupied the U.S. capital.
questions.memory_hook: In 1814, British troops burned the White House and Capitol — Dolley Madison saved a portrait of Washington while fleeing; it remains the only foreign occupation of the US capital.

questions.question: The Haymarket Affair of 1886 became a defining moment in American labor history. What happened in Chicago?
options: Railroad workers went on a nationwide strike that shut down 70% of US freight movement
A bomb exploded at a labor rally in Haymarket Square, killing police officers and protesters, leading to the trial of anarchist labor leaders
Factory workers occupied Carnegie Steel's Chicago plant for 28 days in the first US factory sit-down strike
Labor leaders were assassinated by company-hired Pinkerton guards at a Chicago meatpacking plant
questions.answer: A bomb exploded at a labor rally in Haymarket Square, killing police officers and protesters, leading to the trial of anarchist labor leaders
questions.explanation: On May 4, 1886, a peaceful rally for the 8-hour workday in Haymarket Square turned deadly when an unknown person threw a bomb at police moving to disperse the crowd. Seven police officers and at least four civilians died. Eight anarchist leaders were tried for murder despite no evidence linking them to the bomb; four were hanged. The event set back the American labor movement for years but became a rallying point internationally — May 1st became International Workers' Day to commemorate it.
questions.memory_hook: The 1886 Haymarket bombing — a mystery still unsolved — killed police and civilians at a Chicago labor rally; four anarchists were hanged for it, making May 1st International Workers' Day worldwide.

questions.question: In 1513, Spanish explorer Vasco Núñez de Balboa became the first European to see the Pacific Ocean from the Americas. How did he reach it?
options: He sailed around the southern tip of South America through the strait later named for Magellan
He crossed the Isthmus of Panama on foot through dense jungle
He was guided by Native Americans in canoes across Lake Nicaragua and then to the coast
He sailed north from Peru along the South American coast to discover the ocean
questions.answer: He crossed the Isthmus of Panama on foot through dense jungle
questions.explanation: Balboa led an expedition of about 190 Spaniards and hundreds of Native American guides across the Isthmus of Panama — 80 km of jungle, swamp, and mountains. On September 25, 1513, from a peak in Darién, he became the first European to see the Pacific from the Americas. Four days later he waded into it in full armor, claiming it and all its shores for Spain. He called it the 'South Sea.'
questions.memory_hook: Balboa hacked through Panama's jungle in 1513 to become the first European to see the Pacific — he waded into it in full armor and claimed it for Spain, calling it the 'South Sea.'

questions.question: Joe Louis, 'The Brown Bomber,' held the world heavyweight boxing title for nearly 12 years. What was remarkable about his reign?
options: He defeated 25 consecutive opponents, all by knockout, without a single fight going to decision
He held the heavyweight title for 11 years and 8 months (1937–1949), the longest reign in division history
He won the title at age 17, the youngest heavyweight champion in history
He won 50 consecutive heavyweight title fights across three separate reigns
questions.answer: He held the heavyweight title for 11 years and 8 months (1937–1949), the longest reign in division history
questions.explanation: Louis won the heavyweight title in 1937 at age 23, defeating James Braddock, and defended it a record 25 times — the most of any heavyweight champion — before retiring in 1949. His 1938 rematch victory over German Max Schmeling, whom the Nazi regime had promoted as proof of Aryan superiority, was celebrated as a symbolic blow against fascism. Louis won by knockout in the first round.
questions.memory_hook: Joe Louis held the heavyweight title for nearly 12 years (1937–49) with 25 defenses — his first-round KO of Max Schmeling in 1938 was celebrated worldwide as a blow against Nazi ideology.

questions.question: Martina Navratilova dominated women's tennis in the 1970s–80s. How many Wimbledon singles titles did she win?
questions.explanation: Navratilova won 9 Wimbledon singles titles (1978, 1979, 1982–87, 1990) — the most in the Open Era for either gender until Roger Federer tied it with his 9th in 2017. She was known for her serve-and-volley game and superior athleticism. Overall, Navratilova won 18 Grand Slam singles titles and is widely considered among the greatest players in tennis history.
questions.memory_hook: Navratilova won 9 Wimbledon singles titles — the most in the Open Era until Federer tied her in 2017; her serve-and-volley dominance on grass was virtually unchallenged in the 1980s.

questions.question: Roman Polanski's 1974 neo-noir film Chinatown is set in 1930s Los Angeles. What real historical scandal does its plot draw on?
options: The Black Dahlia murder case of 1947
The California Water Wars — the Owens Valley water theft scandal that built Los Angeles
The Los Angeles Police Department corruption trials of the 1930s
The assassination of a major real-estate developer who controlled LA's growth
questions.answer: The California Water Wars — the Owens Valley water theft scandal that built Los Angeles
questions.explanation: Chinatown, written by Robert Towne and starring Jack Nicholson and Faye Dunaway, is loosely based on the real story of William Mulholland and the Los Angeles Department of Water and Power secretly buying up Owens Valley farmland and diverting the Owens River to LA in the early 1900s — essentially stealing a rural valley's water to enable LA's growth. The film's famous ending — 'Forget it, Jake. It's Chinatown.' — became one of cinema's most quoted lines.
questions.memory_hook: Chinatown (1974) is based on the real water heist that built LA — Mulholland stole the Owens Valley's river water, and the film's final line, 'Forget it, Jake. It's Chinatown,' became shorthand for corruption without justice.

questions.question: Sidney Lumet's 1975 film Dog Day Afternoon, starring Al Pacino, was based on a true event. What was it?
options: A bank robbery in Brooklyn that turned into a hostage crisis and media circus
A hostage negotiation at a New York City hospital taken over by a desperate former patient
A police standoff in Harlem that became a landmark civil rights case
A hijacking of a New York City subway train by a Vietnam veteran
questions.answer: A bank robbery in Brooklyn that turned into a hostage crisis and media circus
questions.explanation: Dog Day Afternoon is based on the August 1972 robbery of the First Brooklyn Savings Bank by John Wojtowicz and Sal Naturale. The robbery went wrong immediately and became a tense day-long hostage situation with television cameras broadcasting live. Wojtowicz's motive was to fund his partner's gender-reassignment surgery — an unexpectedly sympathetic detail that made the crowd outside cheer him on. Pacino won an Oscar nomination for his portrayal.
questions.memory_hook: Dog Day Afternoon (1975) is a true story: a Brooklyn bank robbery to fund a partner's gender surgery turned into a live-TV hostage crisis, with crowds cheering on the robber.

questions.question: Francis Ford Coppola's 1974 thriller The Conversation stars Gene Hackman as a surveillance expert haunted by what he may have recorded. What major award did the film win at Cannes?
options: The Palme d'Or (top prize)
The Grand Prix
The Jury Prize
The FIPRESCI Prize
questions.explanation: The Conversation won the Palme d'Or at the 1974 Cannes Film Festival — the same year Coppola also released The Godfather Part II. The film explores themes of privacy, surveillance, guilt, and paranoia, and was made in the context of the Watergate scandal. Hackman plays Harry Caul, a surveillance professional who believes he has inadvertently recorded evidence of a planned murder.
questions.memory_hook: The Conversation (1974) won Cannes' Palme d'Or — Coppola released it the same year as Godfather Part II, making 1974 perhaps the greatest single year any filmmaker ever had.

questions.question: The Alps are Europe's most prominent mountain range. What is the highest peak in the Alps, and in which country does it sit?
options: The Matterhorn, on the Switzerland-Italy border
Mont Blanc, primarily in France
Monte Rosa, on the Switzerland-Italy border
Grossglockner, in Austria
questions.explanation: Mont Blanc (4,808 m / 15,774 ft) sits on the border between France and Italy, with its summit generally considered to be on the French side. It is the highest peak in both the Alps and Western Europe. The Alps extend approximately 1,200 km across eight countries from France and Monaco in the west to Slovenia and Austria in the east.
questions.memory_hook: Mont Blanc (4,808m) is Europe's highest peak — it sits on the France-Italy border, and the Alps it anchors stretch across eight countries for 1,200 km.

questions.question: The Pyrenees mountain range forms a natural border between two countries. Which two does it separate, and what geographic feature makes this border unusual?
options: France and Spain — the tiny country of Andorra is entirely landlocked within the Pyrenees between them
Spain and Portugal — the mountains run east-west across the Iberian Peninsula
France and Italy — the range extends from the Atlantic to the Mediterranean
Spain and Morocco — the range continues under the Strait of Gibraltar
questions.answer: France and Spain — the tiny country of Andorra is entirely landlocked within the Pyrenees between them
questions.explanation: The Pyrenees stretch about 490 km from the Bay of Biscay (Atlantic) to the Mediterranean Sea, forming the natural land border between France and Spain. Nestled within them is Andorra, a microstate of only 468 km² that has used its mountain location to maintain independence since the 13th century. The range's highest peak is Aneto (3,404 m), on the Spanish side.
questions.memory_hook: The Pyrenees divide France from Spain across 490 km — and tucked inside them is Andorra, a microstate that has leveraged mountain isolation to stay independent since the 1200s.

questions.question: Louis Pasteur's work in the 1850s–60s transformed medicine and food science. What is fermentation, and what did Pasteur prove about it?
options: Fermentation is a purely chemical process; Pasteur proved it required heat to proceed
Fermentation is caused by living microorganisms; Pasteur proved this and refuted the theory of spontaneous generation
Fermentation is the breakdown of proteins; Pasteur discovered the enzymes responsible
Fermentation is a form of oxidation; Pasteur proved oxygen was essential to the process
questions.answer: Fermentation is caused by living microorganisms; Pasteur proved this and refuted the theory of spontaneous generation
questions.explanation: Before Pasteur, fermentation was thought to be a purely chemical process. His experiments in the 1850s showed that specific microorganisms (yeast for alcohol, bacteria for lactic acid) caused fermentation. He also definitively disproved spontaneous generation — the idea that life could arise from non-living matter — through his famous swan-neck flask experiments. His work led directly to pasteurization (heating to kill pathogens) and germ theory of disease.
questions.memory_hook: Pasteur proved fermentation is caused by living microorganisms, not chemistry alone — and his swan-neck flask experiment ended the debate over spontaneous generation forever.

questions.question: Neuroplasticity refers to the brain's ability to change its structure and connections in response to experience. What does this contradict about older views of the brain?
options: That neurons fire electrically — older science believed the brain used chemical signals only
That the adult brain is fixed and unchangeable — older science held that neural connections were essentially permanent after childhood
That different brain regions serve specialized functions — older science believed the brain worked as a unified whole
That the brain consumes significant energy — older science believed thinking used no more energy than sleep
questions.answer: That the adult brain is fixed and unchangeable — older science held that neural connections were essentially permanent after childhood
questions.explanation: For most of the 20th century, neuroscience held that the adult brain was essentially 'hardwired' — neurons could die but not be replaced, and major structural change was impossible. Research since the 1960s, accelerated by stroke rehabilitation studies and later brain imaging, showed that the brain continuously rewires itself in response to learning, injury, and experience. Adults can recover lost functions after strokes and can grow new neurons in the hippocampus well into old age.
questions.memory_hook: Neuroplasticity overturned the 'hardwired adult brain' dogma — stroke victims relearning functions and adults growing new hippocampal neurons proved the brain rewires itself throughout life.
